text: Can I get a {{c1::coffee}} to go, please?
explanation: In everyday speech, native speakers often say 'Can I get...' instead of 'Could I have...' for a casual request. The phrase is common in cafés and shops.
usage:
Pattern: Can I get ...?
  • Can I get a latte?
  • Can I get a table for two?
spanish: ¿Podría pedir un café para llevar, por favor?

text: What would you {{c1::recommend}} from the menu today?
explanation: Recommend is a common polite phrase when asking for a suggestion from a waiter, barista, or host.
usage:
Pattern: What would you recommend?
  • What would you recommend for lunch?
  • What do you recommend for someone who is vegetarian?

scenario: Colloquial English: Special of the Day ⭐
explanation: The special of the day is a restaurant or café promotion or featured dish that the staff recommends.
usage:
Pattern: special of the day / house special
  • What’s the special of the day?
  • The house special is the grilled chicken sandwich.

explanation: In cafés, extra hot means that the drink should be heated more than usual. This phrase is very common in casual service interactions.
usage:
Pattern: extra hot / extra milk / no sugar
  • Could you make it extra hot?
  • Can I get it with oat milk?

explanation: Gluten-free is a common diet-related phrase when asking about food ingredients or allergies.
usage:
Pattern: Is this gluten-free?
  • Is this dish gluten-free?
  • Do you have anything without dairy?

explanation: To split the bill means to divide the total cost between people, especially in restaurants or cafés.
usage:
Pattern: split the bill / separate checks
  • Can we split the bill?
  • Could we get separate checks?

scenario: Colloquial English: That Hit the Spot 😋
explanation: This idiom means the food or drink was exactly what you needed and tasted great.
usage:
Pattern: hit the spot
  • That coffee really hit the spot.
  • The soup hit the spot on a cold day.
spanish: Eso sí que me satisfizo mucho.

scenario: Colloquial English: Cut Back on Sugar 🍰
text: I’m trying to {{c1::cut back on}} sugar these days.
explanation: To cut back on something means to reduce the amount of it. It is a very natural phrase in casual conversation.
usage:
Pattern: cut back on
  • I’m trying to cut back on caffeine.
  • We are cutting back on spending.
spanish: Estoy tratando de reducir el azúcar estos días.
